title: Lacoste Glasses: Styles, Quality, and Where to Buy in Canada
description: Everything you need to know about Lacoste prescription glasses — frame styles, quality, price ranges, and where to find them in Canada.
tldr: Lacoste glasses are mid-range designer eyewear known for clean, sporty-preppy designs. Frames typically run $180-350 and feature the signature crocodile logo. They're widely available at optical stores across Canada.

tldrStats.label: Typical frame price range in Canada

definition.text: Lacoste glasses are prescription and non-prescription eyeglasses produced under license by Marchon Eyewear for the Lacoste fashion brand. Known for their sporty-elegant aesthetic, Lacoste frames feature clean lines, the signature crocodile logo, and materials including acetate, metal, and combination designs. They position in the mid-range designer category.

sections.content:
Lacoste started as a French tennis brand in 1933, founded by tennis player René Lacoste. The brand expanded into eyewear through licensing agreements, and their glasses reflect the same sporty-preppy DNA as their clothing — clean, classic, and slightly athletic in feel.

Lacoste eyewear is manufactured by Marchon, one of the world's largest eyewear licensees. Marchon also produces frames for Calvin Klein, Nike, Columbia, and several other brands. Their manufacturing quality is consistent and reliable across the Lacoste line.

Pricing varies significantly depending on where you buy and what lens features you choose. In Canada, the total cost of a complete pair of glasses (frame + lenses) typically breaks down as follows: budget options start at $150-$250 for basic frames with standard single vision lenses. Mid-range options with anti-reflective coating and a name-brand frame run $300-$500. Premium designer frames with progressive or high-index lenses can reach $600-$900+. The biggest variable is usually the lens — a basic single vision lens costs far less than a digital freeform progressive with premium coatings.

Insurance makes a significant difference. Most employer health plans in Alberta cover $200-$500 toward glasses every 1-2 years. Charm Optical direct-bills most Alberta insurance providers — Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, Desjardins, and more — so you only pay the difference at the counter. No paperwork, no reimbursement wait. Book an eye exam to get your prescription checked and learn exactly what your plan covers before you shop.

Lacoste frames lean toward a clean, sporty-classic aesthetic. You won't find extreme fashion statements here — think polished and versatile.

Men's collection: Rectangular and square frames dominate, with subtle color accents and the crocodile logo on the temple. Materials include matte metal, polished acetate, and combination frames. Colors trend toward blues, greys, blacks, and tortoiseshell.

Women's collection: Cat-eye, round, and rectangular shapes with feminine touches — thinner temples, lighter colours, and occasionally more playful detailing. Rose gold, burgundy, and crystal acetate are popular.

Unisex options: Several models work for any wearer, typically in neutral shapes and colours.

The overall vibe is someone who dresses well without trying too hard — which makes Lacoste frames easy to wear in both professional and casual settings.

Pricing varies significantly depending on where you buy and what lens features you choose. In Canada, the total cost of a complete pair of glasses (frame + lenses) typically breaks down as follows: budget options start at $150-$250 for basic frames with standard single vision lenses. Mid-range options with anti-reflective coating and a name-brand frame run $300-$500. Premium designer frames with progressive or high-index lenses can reach $600-$900+. The biggest variable is usually the lens — a basic single vision lens costs far less than a digital freeform progressive with premium coatings.

Insurance makes a significant difference. Most employer health plans in Alberta cover $200-$500 toward glasses every 1-2 years. Charm Optical direct-bills most Alberta insurance providers — Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, Desjardins, and more — so you only pay the difference at the counter. No paperwork, no reimbursement wait. Book an eye exam to get your prescription checked and learn exactly what your plan covers before you shop.

Lacoste frames sit in the mid-range quality tier — better than budget brands, but not at the level of premium Italian makers like Persol or handmade Japanese frames.

Acetate frames: Good quality plastic with solid colour depth. Hinges are typically standard five-barrel or spring-loaded. The acetate feels substantial without being heavy.

Metal frames: Usually stainless steel or lightweight alloys. Some models use rubber temple tips for a sportier feel. The metal quality is good for the price point.

Details: The crocodile logo is usually a metal appliqué on the temple, sometimes embossed. Colour-matched temple tips and subtle design accents keep things refined.

Durability is solid for everyday wear. With normal care, a pair of Lacoste frames should last 2-4 years before showing significant wear.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

If you're in the Edmonton area, Charm Optical offers comprehensive eye exams that go beyond just measuring your prescription. Their optometrists assess your overall eye health, discuss your visual needs, and recommend lens and frame options tailored to your lifestyle. With direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers and same-day glasses for many prescriptions, getting the right eyewear doesn't have to be complicated or expensive. Book online — appointments are usually available within a few days.

sections.h2: How Much Do Lacoste Glasses Cost?
sections.content:
In Canada, Lacoste frames typically retail for $180-350 (frame only, before lenses). This puts them in the same range as Calvin Klein, Polo Ralph Lauren, and Coach.

Complete pairs (frame + lenses + coatings) will run roughly $350-700 depending on your lens type:

  • Lacoste frame + single vision lenses: $350-500
  • Lacoste frame + progressive lenses: $550-900

With insurance coverage, your out-of-pocket cost can be significantly less. Most Alberta insurance plans cover $200-500 toward frames and lenses combined.

Pricing varies significantly depending on where you buy and what lens features you choose. sections.h2: Where to Buy Lacoste Glasses in Canada
sections.content:
Lacoste frames are distributed through Marchon's network of authorized optical retailers across Canada. You can find them at independent optical stores, some chain retailers, and online.

For the best experience, try Lacoste frames on in person at a local optical store. Online photos don't capture how a frame feels on your face, how the weight distributes, or whether the proportions match your features.

If you're in Edmonton and want to browse similar mid-range designer frames, Charm Optical carries Calvin Klein, Nine West, Polo Ralph Lauren, and other brands in the same style and price category. The team can help you find frames with the same clean, sporty-classic aesthetic that Lacoste is known for.

Pricing varies significantly depending on where you buy and what lens features you choose. In Canada, the total cost of a complete pair of glasses (frame + lenses) typically breaks down as follows: budget options start at $150-$250 for basic frames with standard single vision lenses. Mid-range options with anti-reflective coating and a name-brand frame run $300-$500. Premium designer frames with progressive or high-index lenses can reach $600-$900+. The biggest variable is usually the lens — a basic single vision lens costs far less than a digital freeform progressive with premium coatings.

If you like the Lacoste style but want to compare options, these brands offer a similar vibe:

  • Calvin Klein: Clean, minimalist designs. Slightly more fashion-forward. Similar price range.
  • Polo Ralph Lauren: Classic American preppy styling. Slightly more traditional than Lacoste.
  • Hugo Boss: Similar price point, leaning more corporate/professional.
  • Ray-Ban: Slightly higher price, but the classic styles (Wayfarer, Clubmaster) have a similar timeless quality.

The best approach is trying on frames from several brands side by side. Your face shape, skin tone, and personal preferences will ultimately determine which brand works best for you. Book a visit at Charm Optical and spend some time comparing options — the team is happy to pull frames from different brands to help you find the right match.

Pricing varies significantly depending on where you buy and what lens features you choose. In Canada, the total cost of a complete pair of glasses (frame + lenses) typically breaks down as follows: budget options start at $150-$250 for basic frames with standard single vision lenses. Mid-range options with anti-reflective coating and a name-brand frame run $300-$500. Premium designer frames with progressive or high-index lenses can reach $600-$900+. The biggest variable is usually the lens — a basic single vision lens costs far less than a digital freeform progressive with premium coatings.

A well-fitting frame does more than look good — it ensures your lenses perform correctly. Frames that sit too high or low shift the optical centres away from your pupils, causing blurry vision and eye strain. Frames that are too wide slide down your nose, while tight frames create pressure headaches behind the ears.

Three measurements define frame fit: lens width (the horizontal size of each lens opening), bridge width (the gap between the two lenses), and temple length (the arm that hooks behind your ear). These are printed on the inside of the temple arm as three numbers like 52-18-140. Your optician uses these alongside your face measurements to recommend frames that balance aesthetics and function.

This is why in-person fitting matters, especially for progressive lenses where vertical lens alignment is critical. At Charm Optical in Edmonton, the opticians take precise measurements using digital tools to ensure your frames and lenses work together perfectly. With over 500 frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, you'll find the right combination of style and fit. Most styles are available for same-day glasses at Charm Optical with single vision lenses.

Your eyes are one of the most important tools you have, and they deserve proper care and correction. The technology behind modern eyewear has advanced remarkably in recent years — from digital freeform progressive lenses that customize the optics to your exact wearing position, to lightweight titanium frames that weigh less than a coin, to anti-reflective coatings that virtually eliminate glare. Understanding these options empowers you to make better choices about your eye health and get glasses that genuinely improve your quality of life rather than just meeting a basic need.

Regular eye exams are the foundation of good vision care. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for adults and annually for children, seniors over 65, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease. A comprehensive exam does far more than measure your prescription — your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels, catching conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration in their earliest stages. Many serious eye conditions have no noticeable symptoms until significant damage has occurred, making preventive exams essential.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. They offer direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers, so you only pay the difference at the counter.

Whether you are buying your first pair of glasses, upgrading to progressive lenses, or just need an adjustment on your current frames, having a trusted local optician makes the process smoother. Book an appointment online — most appointments are available within a few days, and walk-ins are welcome for adjustments and frame browsing.

faqs.a: Yes, for their price range. Lacoste frames are manufactured by Marchon, a reputable eyewear company, and use solid materials. They're a reliable mid-range choice — not budget, not luxury, but well-made for everyday wear.
Lacoste eyewear is produced under license by Marchon Eyewear, one of the world's largest eyewear manufacturers. Marchon also makes frames for Calvin Klein, Nike, and Columbia.

faqs.q: Can I get Lacoste glasses with progressive lenses?
faqs.a: Yes. Lacoste frames can be fitted with any lens type — single vision, bifocal, or progressive. Most Lacoste frames have enough vertical height to work well with progressive lenses. Ask your optician to confirm compatibility.

faqs.q: Where can I try on Lacoste-style glasses in Edmonton?
faqs.a: Charm Optical on Ellerslie Road in South Edmonton carries multiple brands in the same style and price category as Lacoste, including Calvin Klein and Polo Ralph Lauren. The team can help you find the sporty-classic look you're after.

faqs.q: How do I know my glasses frame size?
faqs.a: Look inside the temple arm for three numbers (e.g., 52-18-140). The first is lens width in mm, second is bridge width, third is temple length. If you don't have a current pair, an optician at Charm Optical can measure your face to determine the ideal frame dimensions.

faqs.q: What frame material is most durable?
faqs.a: Titanium is the most durable frame material — it's lightweight, corrosion-resistant, hypoallergenic, and flexes without breaking. For budget durability, TR-90 nylon is nearly indestructible. Acetate offers the best colour variety. Charm Optical carries all three materials across their 500+ frame collection.
